text: Hello, I'm sharing a robotic anterior resection for a sigmoid cancer with malignant fistula to the bladder. This is a patient who had multiple UTIs, was thought to have a diverticular associated fistula. CAT scan showed a mass in the sigmoid, which was corroborated on colonoscopy, and he presents for resection.
The surgeon begins a medial to lateral dissection, searching for the ureter while using stents and ICG. The right pelvic peritoneum is visibly drawn up into the tumor, along with the appendix.

The surgeon traces along the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), identifying bulky lymph nodes at the junction with the aorta. The surgeon aims to excise these lymph nodes while preserving the surrounding nerves.
The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) while considering the patient's calcified mesenteric vessels and abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) visible on the CAT scan.

The surgeon performs a medial dissection, carefully preserving nerves while isolating the superior hemorrhoidal artery just distal to the take-off of the left colic artery. The vessel is prepared for sealing using a vessel sealer.

The surgeon continues the medial dissection, identifying the ureter before proceeding to ligate the left colic artery. The dissection progresses from medial to lateral, completing the separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon identifies the molybdenum fistula to the bladder and the appendix, deciding to remove them en bloc. The procedure continues with lateral dissection before addressing the fistula.

The surgeon completes the medial dissection, clearly visualizing the ureter while progressing towards the area of adherence to the fistula. The surrounding tissue appears inflamed, suggesting an inflammatory reaction rather than malignancy.

The surgeon marks the junction between the colon and bladder, then begins to incise into the bladder. As the incision progresses, a large exophytic frond-like tumor becomes visible, leading to some fragmentation. The surgeon carefully enters the mucosa of the bladder, exposing the tumor.

The surgeon visualizes the interior of the bladder, facilitating the excision of the tumor margin. The large frond-like tumor is visible at the dome of the bladder. The surgeon carefully cauterizes tissue on the left side, ensuring the ureter, which is visibly distant, is not at risk.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the posterior wall of the bladder, revealing the Foley catheter and ureteral stents in the background. The dissection progresses along the most inferior posterior margin of the bladder.

The surgeon performs a distal division of the bowel, followed by transection of the proximal mesentery, avoiding a small diverticulum by going proximal to it. A fan and steel incision is made to extract the specimen and clean up tumor debris, preparing for bladder repair in an open fashion.

The surgeon mobilizes the bladder completely below the pubic tubercle, ensuring it is free for extraction through a fan and steel incision. A direct hernia is visible on the right side. The specimen is extracted, and the bladder is repaired in layers using PDS and serosal silk sutures. The surgeon returns to robotic assistance to perform the sononastomosis.

The surgeon sutures the stapled ends of the bowel together using a minimally invasive approach, followed by creating a proctotomy and colotomy. The inner back row is sewn towards the patient's left side, with ICG checks performed for verification. The robotic system is utilized for precision in the sewn anastomosis.

In these cases where a large bladder repair is done, I will leave a Foley catheter, I will leave a pelvic drain, and then typically about five to seven days later, I will get a retrograde cystogram and check the bladder integrity before I remove the Foley catheter. The drain is left in place in the event that high output is noted and it could signify a bladder leak before you even do your study, but also in the event that the cystogram proves that there is an occult leak that you didn't know about, you already have a drain in place and you're fully in place that you would just leave for a little bit longer, presuming you didn't need to do any further surgical repair. Although I did have urology involved in the case to do the stents for this procedure, I did the bladder repair myself. It's really no different than sewing bowel together, honestly. So we'll come around the anteriorly and I do do an imbrogating canal suture for this.

The surgeon demonstrates a wide open anastomosis, with the inner layer sutures on the outer row being crossed and tied together. The original suture used for sewing the staple lines is run over the top to imbricate the anterior row, completing the two-layer anastomosis. The suture is tied to the original stay suture for added security, followed by a leak test.

The surgeon performs a leak test on the repaired bladder by instilling methylene blue in saline up to 300 cc's, observing no leakage. The stents are removed, ensuring no encroachment on the ureter orifices, while the Foley catheter remains in place.
